heroOverview: Insomnia is a sustained difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or achieving restorative sleep that impairs daytime functioning — affecting an estimated 10–15% of adults with a chronic pattern and up to 30% in any given year. Unlike transient sleeplessness tied to a single stressor, unspecified insomnia (ICD-10: G47.00) persists for at least three nights per week over at least three months in most diagnostic frameworks. The condition sits at the intersection of neurology, psychiatry, and sleep medicine: disordered arousal and heightened physiological hyperactivation — not simply a deficit of sleepiness — are the central mechanisms. Chronic insomnia doubles the risk of major depression and is independently associated with hypertension, metabolic syndrome, and reduced immune function. Effective, non-pharmacological treatment exists in the form of cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I), which produces durable remission in roughly 70–80% of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Chronic insomnia affects 10–15% of adults worldwide; transient sleep difficulty affects up to 30% annually (AASM, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: More prevalent in women (approximately 1.4:1 female-to-male ratio), older adults, and those with comorbid psychiatric disorders
keyStats.avgAge: Risk rises progressively after age 45 and is highest in adults over 65
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 300–400 million adults worldwide meet criteria for chronic insomnia disorder

definition: Insomnia, unspecified (ICD-10: G47.00) is a sleep disorder characterised by subjective complaints of difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early-morning awakening combined with significant daytime consequences — fatigue, impaired concentration, mood disturbance, or functional impairment — that cannot be better explained by another sleep disorder, a general medical condition, or a substance. The 'unspecified' designation is used when the clinical picture meets threshold criteria for insomnia disorder but subtype classification (e.g., psychophysiological, idiopathic, paradoxical) has not been assigned. International guidelines from the American Academy of Sleep Medicine and the DSM-5 require that the sleep difficulty occur at least three nights per week, persist for at least three months, and cause clinically significant distress or impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity. The underlying pathophysiology centres on hyperarousal: elevated cortisol secretion, increased metabolic rate during sleep, and heightened high-frequency electroencephalographic activity that keeps the brain in a lighter, more reactive state. Insomnia is classified under neurology and sleep medicine and is the most frequently reported sleep complaint in primary care worldwide.

typesClassification.description: Sleep difficulty lasting fewer than three months, typically triggered by a stressor such as illness, bereavement, or job change; usually self-limiting but can progress to chronic insomnia if maladaptive sleep behaviours develop.

Meets the three-nights-per-week, three-months-duration criteria; driven by perpetuating factors like conditioned arousal, anxiety about sleep, and irregular schedules rather than the original precipitant.
Heightened somatic tension and learned association between the bedroom environment and wakefulness; sufferers often report sleeping better away from their own bed.
A mismatch between subjective complaint and objective sleep duration; patients report severe insomnia while polysomnography shows near-normal sleep, reflecting misperception of sleep state.
Insomnia occurring alongside and exacerbated by another medical or psychiatric disorder (e.g., depression, chronic pain, restless legs) but warranting independent treatment.

primarySymptoms: Prolonged sleep onset latency — lying awake for 30 minutes or longer before falling asleep despite feeling tired.
Frequent night-time awakenings — waking two or more times per night and struggling to fall back asleep within 20–30 minutes.
Early-morning awakening — waking an hour or more before the desired or planned time and being unable to return to sleep.
Unrefreshing or non-restorative sleep — waking feeling as though no rest was obtained even after an apparently adequate time in bed.
Daytime fatigue and low energy — a persistent heaviness and reduced physical and cognitive stamina directly attributable to poor night-time sleep.
Impaired attention, concentration, and working memory — difficulty holding information in mind, making decisions, or completing complex tasks at the usual level.
Mood disturbance — irritability, low mood, emotional reactivity, or heightened anxiety that tracks the severity of sleep disruption.
Increased anxiety about sleep itself — monitoring the clock, anticipatory dread as bedtime approaches, and ruminative worry that poor sleep will cause harm.
Tension headaches or gastrointestinal discomfort on waking, reflecting the physiological hyperarousal state.
Reduced motivation and performance at work or school, with increased errors and slowed reaction time.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticeably longer time to fall asleep than usual for two or more consecutive nights.
Waking once and finding it harder than normal to return to sleep.
Starting to check the clock repeatedly during the night.
A subtle increase in pre-bed worry or physical tension that was not previously present.
emergencySigns: Severe sleep deprivation causing hallucinations, confusion, or disorientation — requires same-day assessment.
Suicidal thoughts or self-harm ideation that emerge or worsen in the context of prolonged insomnia.
Insomnia accompanied by chest pain, palpitations, or breathing difficulty that suggests an undiagnosed medical cause.
Complete inability to sleep for more than 48–72 consecutive hours in the absence of prior diagnosis.

causes.description: Repeated nights of wakefulness associate the bedroom and bed with alertness rather than sleep; the brain learns to activate when approaching sleep, a classical conditioning process that sustains insomnia long after the original trigger resolves.

Cognitive rumination and worry activate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, raising cortisol and norepinephrine late into the night, preventing the neurological transition from wakefulness to NREM sleep.

Reduced slow-wave sleep and REM sleep abnormalities are core biological features of major depression; insomnia and depression maintain each other bidirectionally, with insomnia raising the risk of depression roughly twofold.

Shift work, transmeridian travel, and inconsistent bed and wake times desynchronise the circadian clock from the sleep homeostatic drive, fragmenting sleep architecture and reducing subjective sleep quality.

Caffeine consumed within six hours of bedtime extends sleep onset latency by blocking adenosine receptors; alcohol suppresses REM sleep and causes rebound awakenings in the second half of the night; nicotine is a direct arousal agent.

Chronic pain, nocturnal asthma, gastro-oesophageal reflux, nocturia from prostate disease or heart failure, and hyperthyroidism all fragment sleep by generating arousals or discomfort that the sleeping brain cannot ignore.

A bedroom that is too warm (optimum core body temperature drop of ~1°C is needed to initiate sleep), excessively lit (blue-spectrum light suppresses melatonin secretion), or noisy disrupts the physiological conditions required for sleep onset and maintenance.

riskFactors.description: Women are approximately 40% more likely than men to report chronic insomnia; hormonal fluctuations across the menstrual cycle, pregnancy, and the menopausal transition contribute through both biological and psychosocial pathways.

Sleep architecture shifts with ageing — slow-wave sleep decreases, sleep efficiency falls, and circadian rhythm amplitude diminishes; adults over 65 report insomnia at rates roughly double those of young adults.

Approximately 40% of people with insomnia disorder have a comorbid psychiatric diagnosis; depression, generalised anxiety, PTSD, and bipolar disorder each independently predict more severe and treatment-resistant insomnia.
Work overload, relationship conflict, and financial strain activate the stress response and elevate pre-sleep arousal; stress-reduction techniques and CBT address this pathway directly.
Night-shift workers have roughly 2–3 times the insomnia prevalence of day workers because of the chronic mismatch between occupational demands and the biological circadian rhythm.
Both are modifiable through behavioural change; limiting caffeine to before noon and avoiding alcohol within three hours of bedtime measurably reduces sleep latency and wake-after-sleep-onset.

Chronic pain, cardiovascular disease, respiratory disease, and neurological conditions each raise insomnia prevalence two- to threefold through nocturnal symptom burden and associated psychological distress.

Physical inactivity reduces homeostatic sleep pressure; regular moderate aerobic exercise has demonstrated modest but consistent improvements in sleep onset latency and total sleep time in randomised trials.

affectedDemographics: Women across all age groups, with higher prevalence during perimenopause and menopause
Adults over 65, in whom both primary and comorbid insomnia are very common
Shift workers and healthcare professionals with irregular schedules
Individuals with depression, anxiety disorders, or PTSD
People with chronic pain conditions or cardiovascular or respiratory disease
Students and high-stress professionals during periods of sustained cognitive demand
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of insomnia is clinical and rests on a thorough sleep history. The clinician assesses the three core domains: difficulty with sleep initiation, maintenance, or early awakening; adequate sleep opportunity; and resulting daytime impairment. Sleep diary data collected over one to two weeks — logging bed time, estimated sleep onset latency, number and duration of awakenings, wake time, and daytime functioning — provides objective-adjacent evidence that neither over- nor underestimates the problem the way single-point recall does. Standardised questionnaires such as the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) and Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) quantify symptom severity and track treatment response. The clinical interview must also screen for comorbid conditions that can cause or worsen insomnia: depression, anxiety, restless legs syndrome, obstructive sleep apnoea, periodic limb movement disorder, thyroid dysfunction, chronic pain, and substance use. Polysomnography is not recommended for the routine evaluation of insomnia but is indicated when the clinical picture suggests a concurrent sleep disorder such as sleep apnoea or nocturnal movement disorder. Actigraphy — a wrist-worn accelerometer worn for one to two weeks — can objectively characterise the sleep-wake pattern and is useful when the sleep diary and history are inconsistent or when circadian rhythm disorder is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Captures bed time, sleep onset latency, night awakenings, final wake time, and daytime functioning across multiple nights, providing a more accurate picture than retrospective recall.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You record sleep and wake times each morning and evening; the diary takes two to three minutes per day and is reviewed by the clinician at the next appointment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A validated seven-item questionnaire scoring the severity of sleep-onset, sleep-maintenance, and early-waking difficulties, along with satisfaction, interference, and worry about sleep.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief paper or online questionnaire taking under five minutes; scores above 14 suggest moderate to severe insomnia disorder.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides an objective, day-by-day estimate of sleep-wake timing and total sleep time over one to two weeks, detecting circadian misalignment and quantifying sleep efficiency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You wear a watch-like device on the non-dominant wrist continuously for up to two weeks and then return it for clinician download and analysis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Overnight laboratory sleep study measuring brainwaves, eye movements, muscle tone, heart rate, and breathing; used when a comorbid sleep disorder (e.g., sleep apnoea, periodic limb movement disorder) is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You sleep in a clinical suite with electrodes attached; the procedure is painless and takes one night, with results reviewed by a sleep specialist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hyperthyroidism, which can directly cause persistent insomnia and anxiety; recommended when other signs of thyroid disease are present.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; results are usually available within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Screening questionnaires for mood and anxiety (PHQ-9, GAD-7)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the severity of comorbid depression and generalised anxiety, which co-occur with insomnia in up to 40% of cases and require parallel treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Short self-completed questionnaires (nine and seven items respectively) taking two to three minutes each at the clinic visit.

treatmentOverview: Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) is the first-line treatment recommended by all major sleep medicine and psychiatric guidelines, including the AASM, NICE, and the European Sleep Research Society. CBT-I combines five evidence-based components: sleep restriction (compressing time in bed to build sleep pressure), stimulus control (re-associating the bed with sleep rather than wakefulness), sleep hygiene education, cognitive restructuring (challenging catastrophic beliefs about sleep), and relaxation training. Across randomised controlled trials, CBT-I achieves clinically meaningful reduction in sleep onset latency and wake-after-sleep-onset in roughly 70–80% of patients, with remission rates comparable to or exceeding pharmacotherapy. Effects are durable: improvements are maintained at 12-month and 24-month follow-up in most studies. Digital CBT-I programmes and therapist-delivered group CBT-I are effective alternatives when face-to-face access is limited. Pharmacotherapy — including the orexin receptor antagonist suvorexant, the melatonin receptor agonist ramelteon, low-dose doxepin, and the non-benzodiazepine hypnotics eszopiclone and zolpidem — is reserved for short-term adjunctive use or when CBT-I is unavailable or has failed. Benzodiazepines are not recommended long-term because of dependency risk and next-day impairment. Melatonin has limited evidence for sleep-onset insomnia in older adults and in circadian delay disorders but modest efficacy for primary insomnia. Any comorbid condition — depression, pain, sleep apnoea — must be treated alongside insomnia-specific therapy for optimal outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I)
medicalTreatments.description: A structured 6–8 session programme combining sleep restriction, stimulus control, cognitive restructuring, sleep hygiene, and relaxation; delivered in person, by telephone, or via validated digital platforms. It is the treatment most strongly recommended across all international guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clinically significant improvement in 70–80% of patients; remission of insomnia disorder maintained at 12-month follow-up in approximately 50–60% of treated individuals.

medicalTreatments.name: Suvorexant (orexin receptor antagonist)
medicalTreatments.description: Blocks orexin-1 and orexin-2 receptors to reduce wakefulness drive rather than sedating; effective for both sleep-onset and sleep-maintenance insomnia. Approved for chronic insomnia in adults.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces wake-after-sleep-onset by approximately 28–38 minutes versus placebo in 3-month phase III trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose doxepin (3–6 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: A tricyclic antidepressant at sub-antidepressant doses that selectively blocks histamine H1 receptors; specifically approved for sleep-maintenance insomnia in adults.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significantly reduces wake-after-sleep-onset and improves total sleep time versus placebo, with effect sizes of roughly 0.5–0.8 in clinical trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (eszopiclone, zolpidem, zaleplon)
medicalTreatments.description: GABA-A positive allosteric modulators with shorter half-lives than benzodiazepines; used for short-term relief of severe insomnia when CBT-I is being established. Risk of dependence, tolerance, and next-day impairment exists with extended use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce sleep onset latency by approximately 10–20 minutes and improve subjective sleep quality versus placebo; tolerance typically develops within 2–4 weeks of nightly use.

medicalTreatments.name: Ramelteon (melatonin receptor agonist)
medicalTreatments.description: Activates MT1 and MT2 melatonin receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus; most effective for sleep-onset delay and circadian rhythm-component insomnia. Low dependency potential.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces sleep onset latency by approximately 7–12 minutes versus placebo; particularly useful in older adults, where it reduces next-day impairment risk compared with GABA-acting hypnotics.

medicalTreatments.name: Melatonin (low-dose, exogenous)
medicalTreatments.description: 0.5–2 mg taken 30–60 minutes before the desired sleep time; most evidenced for circadian phase delay and in adults over 55. Not recommended as a standalone treatment for chronic sleep-maintenance insomnia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show modest reductions in sleep onset latency (approximately 7 minutes on average) and marginal total sleep time improvements; effect sizes smaller than CBT-I or approved hypnotics.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)
alternativeTreatments.description: Eight-week group or digital programme training present-moment awareness; shown in randomised trials to reduce pre-sleep arousal and improve sleep quality, with modest but consistent effect sizes in adults with chronic insomnia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Some randomised trials report reduced sleep onset latency and improved sleep efficiency; evidence quality is moderate and results are inconsistent, limiting its endorsement as first-line care, but it is a low-risk adjunct for patients who prefer non-pharmacological approaches.

alternativeTreatments.name: Progressive muscle relaxation and biofeedback
alternativeTreatments.description: Systematic tension and release of muscle groups reduces somatic arousal before sleep; forms one component of CBT-I and is effective as a stand-alone technique for people whose insomnia is driven primarily by physical tension.

whySeeSpecialist: A neurologist or sleep medicine specialist should be involved when insomnia has persisted for more than six months despite primary-care management, when CBT-I has failed to achieve remission, when polysomnography is needed to rule out sleep apnoea or periodic limb movements, or when the sleep history suggests a circadian rhythm disorder. Specialist input ensures accurate subtype diagnosis, access to structured CBT-I programmes, and safe titration of pharmacotherapy in complex cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a sleep medicine physician, neurologist, or psychologist accredited in behavioural sleep medicine who has specific training in CBT-I delivery. For pharmacological management in older adults or those with comorbidities, a geriatrician or psychiatrist with sleep medicine experience is valuable. Ask whether the practice offers structured CBT-I (not just sleep hygiene advice), has access to actigraphy or polysomnography, and how comorbid conditions such as depression or sleep apnoea are co-managed.
preventionStrategies: Maintain a consistent bed and wake time seven days a week, even after poor nights, to anchor the circadian clock.
Limit caffeine intake after noon and avoid alcohol within three hours of bedtime.
Reserve the bed exclusively for sleep and intimacy; working, watching screens, and lying awake worrying in bed reinforces wakefulness.
Establish a 30-minute wind-down routine — dim lights, reduce screen use, and reduce cognitive demands — before the planned sleep time.
Treat comorbid depression, anxiety, and pain early, as untreated psychological distress is the most powerful precipitant of chronic insomnia.
Address shift-work demands with strategic light exposure and napping protocols to minimise circadian disruption.
lifestyleModifications: Perform moderate aerobic exercise on most days of the week, but avoid vigorous exercise within two to three hours of bedtime.
Keep the bedroom cool (around 18°C), dark, and quiet; blackout curtains and white-noise machines address environmental barriers.
Avoid clock-watching during the night — turn clocks away from view, as time-monitoring amplifies arousal.
If unable to sleep after 20 minutes, leave the bed and engage in calm, non-screen activity until sleepy, then return.
Manage stress with structured daily worry time — writing concerns and to-do lists earlier in the evening rather than processing them in bed.
Avoid long or late daytime naps, which reduce homeostatic sleep pressure and make night-time sleep harder.

recommended: Tryptophan-containing foods such as turkey, milk, nuts, and seeds, which provide a substrate for serotonin and melatonin synthesis
Complex carbohydrates at dinner, which modestly increase tryptophan availability to the brain
Magnesium-rich foods such as leafy greens, almonds, and whole grains, which support muscle relaxation and GABA activity
Adequate hydration throughout the day to avoid nocturnal thirst without drinking large amounts in the evening
avoid: Caffeine after noon — it has a half-life of 5–6 hours and delays sleep onset measurably
Alcohol within three hours of bedtime — suppresses REM sleep and causes rebound arousals in the early hours
Large, heavy meals within two hours of bedtime, which raise core body temperature and can worsen gastro-oesophageal reflux
High-sugar foods in the evening, which cause blood glucose fluctuations that may provoke night-time awakening

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise — brisk walking, swimming, or cycling for 30 minutes on at least five days per week — is one of the most consistently supported non-pharmacological interventions for sleep quality. A meta-analysis of 17 randomised trials found exercise reduced sleep onset latency by approximately 13 minutes and increased total sleep time by 18 minutes versus control. The timing matters: morning or afternoon exercise benefits sleep without the arousal-elevating effect of vigorous late-evening workouts. Resistance training also improves sleep quality in older adults. Even 10-minute bouts of moderate activity accumulate benefit over the day.
dailyManagement: Keep a consistent wake time every morning regardless of how much you slept the night before — this is the single most powerful circadian anchor.
Complete a sleep diary daily during active treatment to monitor patterns and guide therapy adjustments.
Practice stimulus control: if awake in bed for more than 20 minutes, get up and do something quiet until sleepy.
Reduce screen exposure — especially smartphone use — in the 60 minutes before your target sleep time.
Apply cognitive restructuring techniques when catastrophic thoughts about sleep arise; challenge the belief that one bad night causes permanent harm.
Re-evaluate pharmacotherapy at every review — hypnotics should not be taken nightly long-term without periodic reassessment.
prognosis: With evidence-based treatment, the outlook for insomnia disorder is favourable. CBT-I produces clinically meaningful improvement in approximately 70–80% of patients and remission of insomnia disorder in 50–60% at 12-month follow-up. Short-term pharmacotherapy provides rapid symptom relief in most patients but does not address underlying perpetuating factors. Without treatment, chronic insomnia persists for years in the majority of sufferers: natural history studies show that approximately 40–70% of adults with insomnia still meet criteria at one-year follow-up. Comorbid psychiatric disorders substantially worsen prognosis and must be treated concurrently. The cardiovascular and metabolic consequences of long-standing sleep disruption — elevated hypertension risk, impaired glucose tolerance, and increased cortisol burden — are among the strongest arguments for early, active management rather than watchful waiting.
recoveryTimeline: Patients beginning CBT-I typically see measurable improvements in sleep diary metrics within two to three weeks of consistent practice, with the greatest gains occurring during weeks four to six as sleep restriction and stimulus control take effect. Full remission, defined as normalisation of sleep onset latency and wake-after-sleep-onset to under 30 minutes with improved daytime function, is usually achieved by session six to eight (weeks six to eight of structured therapy). Pharmacotherapy produces faster initial improvement — often within the first week — but improvements plateau and may reverse on discontinuation. Patients using combined CBT-I and short-term medication typically transition off the medication by week four to six as behavioural gains consolidate.
complications: Major depressive disorder — chronic insomnia approximately doubles the risk of a first depressive episode; the relationship is bidirectional and requires parallel treatment.
Generalised anxiety disorder — persistent hyperarousal associated with insomnia and anxiety share overlapping neurobiological pathways, and each condition amplifies the other.
Cardiovascular disease — longitudinal studies show that sleeping fewer than six hours per night is associated with a 20–48% increased risk of hypertension, coronary artery disease, and heart failure.
Impaired immune function — chronic sleep restriction reduces natural killer cell activity and antibody response to vaccination, increasing susceptibility to infections.
Motor vehicle accidents — sleep-deprived drivers have reaction times comparable to drivers with a 0.08% blood-alcohol level; insomnia is associated with a two- to fivefold increased crash risk.
Occupational impairment and reduced quality of life — absenteeism, presenteeism, and cognitive deficits generate significant individual and societal productivity losses.

supportResources.name: AASM Sleep Education — Insomnia
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of insomnia symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment from the American Academy of Sleep Medicine.

supportResources.name: NHS — Insomnia
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on insomnia causes, self-help strategies, and when to seek medical advice.

supportResources.name: NIH MedlinePlus — Insomnia
supportResources.description: Comprehensive resource linking patients to guidelines, clinical trials, and authoritative information on insomnia.

sources.title: Qaseem A et al. Management of Chronic Insomnia Disorder in Adults: A Clinical Practice Guideline from the American College of Physicians. Annals of Internal Medicine, 2016
Riemann D et al. European guideline for the diagnosis and treatment of insomnia. Journal of Sleep Research, 2017
Trauer JM et al. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Chronic Insomnia: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Annals of Internal Medicine, 2015
NICE CG159 — Chronic primary insomnia: misuse of and dependence on hypnotics and anxiolytics
Sateia MJ et al. Clinical Practice Guideline for the Pharmacologic Treatment of Chronic Insomnia in Adults. Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine, 2017
WHO — Mental health: Strengthening our response (sleep and mental health)
Kessler RC et al. Insomnia and the performance of US workers: results from the American Insomnia Survey. Sleep, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnoea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sleep apnoea causes sleep disruption through repeated breathing pauses and oxygen desaturations; patients usually snore loudly and are unaware of awakenings, unlike the conscious, anxious wakefulness of insomnia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary insomnia (psychophysiological)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary insomnia has a clearly identified psychophysiological mechanism (conditioned arousal); G47.00 is an unspecified category used before formal subtype assessment is complete.

confusedWithConditions.name: Major depressive disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression consistently disrupts sleep but is distinguished by pervasive low mood, anhedonia, and neurovegetative symptoms beyond sleep; insomnia alone does not meet diagnostic criteria for depression.

confusedWithConditions.name: Generalised anxiety disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GAD causes chronic worry and hyperarousal that impair sleep, but the core symptom is excessive anxiety across multiple domains; insomnia disorder is diagnosed when sleep difficulty is the primary presenting complaint.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnoea
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common comorbid sleep disorder; untreated sleep apnoea fragments sleep and can sustain or worsen insomnia, requiring concurrent management.

relatedConditions.name: Major depressive disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Insomnia is both a symptom and a risk factor for depression; the two conditions share neurobiological substrates and often require integrated treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Generalised anxiety disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Persistent anxiety and hyperarousal are the primary perpetuating mechanisms of chronic insomnia; CBT-I directly addresses anxiety about sleep.

relatedConditions.name: Shift work sleep disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Occupational circadian misalignment produces insomnia phenotypes and requires specialised circadian management strategies alongside standard CBT-I.

relatedConditions.name: Bipolar disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Sleep disturbance is a prodrome of both manic and depressive episodes in bipolar disorder; insomnia monitoring is part of mood-episode early-warning systems.

faqs.question: What qualifies as insomnia rather than just a few bad nights?
faqs.answer: Clinical insomnia requires difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early at least three nights per week for at least three months, combined with daytime impairment. Occasional poor sleep tied to a specific stressor is normal and not classified as insomnia disorder.

faqs.question: What is the most effective treatment for chronic insomnia?
faqs.answer: Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) is the first-line treatment recommended by all major guidelines. It improves sleep in 70–80% of patients with durable results at one year, outperforming medication for long-term outcomes.

faqs.question: Is insomnia a mental health condition?
faqs.answer: Insomnia sits at the boundary of neurology, sleep medicine, and psychiatry. It involves biological hyperarousal and psychological perpetuating factors. It is not simply anxiety or depression but frequently co-occurs with them and benefits from psychologically informed treatment such as CBT-I.

faqs.question: Can insomnia be cured permanently?
faqs.answer: Approximately 50–60% of patients achieve sustained remission after a full course of CBT-I, with benefits maintained at one year. Some individuals have recurrences during high-stress periods but often respond rapidly to brief CBT-I refresher sessions.

faqs.question: Why do I keep waking up at 3am?
faqs.answer: Waking between 2 and 4 am is the most common maintenance insomnia pattern. It often reflects a rebound in cortisol secretion, the body's natural early-morning arousal signal arriving prematurely, sometimes exacerbated by alcohol, stress, or pain.

faqs.question: How does sleep restriction therapy work?
faqs.answer: Sleep restriction temporarily limits time in bed to match the actual time spent sleeping, building up strong homeostatic sleep pressure. As sleep efficiency improves above 85–90%, time in bed is gradually extended by 15-minute increments until the patient reaches their target sleep duration.

faqs.question: Are sleeping pills safe for long-term use?
faqs.answer: Standard hypnotics such as zolpidem and benzodiazepines are not recommended for long-term nightly use due to dependency risk, tolerance, rebound insomnia on stopping, and next-day impairment. Orexin antagonists and low-dose doxepin have better long-term safety profiles but are still reviewed periodically.

faqs.question: Does melatonin help insomnia?
faqs.answer: Melatonin has modest evidence for reducing sleep onset latency by around 7 minutes on average and is most useful for sleep-phase delay, jet lag, and insomnia in adults over 55. Its effect on sleep-maintenance insomnia is limited compared to CBT-I or approved hypnotics.

faqs.question: Can exercise improve insomnia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Moderate aerobic exercise reduces sleep onset latency by approximately 13 minutes and increases total sleep time by about 18 minutes based on meta-analyses. Morning or afternoon exercise is preferred; vigorous exercise within 2–3 hours of bedtime can delay sleep onset.

faqs.question: What is stimulus control therapy for insomnia?
faqs.answer: Stimulus control retrains the brain to associate the bed with sleep rather than wakefulness by restricting bed use to sleep and sex, getting up when unable to sleep within 20 minutes, and keeping a consistent wake time daily.

faqs.question: How does insomnia affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Sleeping fewer than six hours per night is associated with a 20–48% increased risk of hypertension and elevated coronary artery disease risk in large longitudinal studies. Chronic sleep loss raises sympathetic nervous system activity, cortisol, and inflammatory markers that drive cardiovascular risk.

faqs.question: Is insomnia genetic?
faqs.answer: Twin studies estimate heritability of insomnia symptoms at approximately 40–57%. Several genome-wide association studies have identified loci associated with insomnia, many overlapping with depression and anxiety pathways. Genetic predisposition interacts with environmental and behavioural triggers.

faqs.question: Why is insomnia worse during menopause?
faqs.answer: Declining oestrogen and progesterone during the menopausal transition reduce sleep-promoting effects of these hormones, increase hot flushes that fragment sleep, and heighten HPA axis reactivity. Women in perimenopause have insomnia rates roughly double those of premenopausal peers.

faqs.question: Can insomnia cause weight gain?
faqs.answer: Chronic sleep restriction disrupts ghrelin and leptin regulation, increasing hunger (particularly for high-calorie foods) and reducing satiety signals. Short sleep duration is associated with a 55% higher obesity risk in adults in large meta-analyses. Poor sleep also reduces motivation to exercise.

faqs.question: What foods help with sleep?
faqs.answer: Tryptophan-rich foods (turkey, milk, nuts), magnesium-rich foods (leafy greens, almonds), and complex carbohydrates at dinner support sleep biochemistry. Avoiding caffeine after noon and alcohol close to bedtime has the strongest evidence for improving sleep quality.

faqs.question: Is it safe to drive with insomnia?
faqs.answer: Chronic insomnia impairs reaction time and decision-making comparably to alcohol intoxication. The risk of a motor vehicle accident is 2–5 times higher in people with insomnia. Patients with severe daytime sleepiness or cognitive impairment should be counselled about driving safety.

faqs.question: When should I see a specialist for insomnia?
faqs.answer: See a sleep medicine specialist or neurologist if insomnia has persisted for more than 6 months despite primary care management, if CBT-I has not helped, if symptoms suggest sleep apnoea or a movement disorder, or if significant daytime impairment is affecting safety or work.

faqs.question: Can CBT-I be done online?
faqs.answer: Yes. Several validated digital CBT-I programmes (such as Sleepio) have demonstrated efficacy comparable to therapist-delivered CBT-I in randomised trials, with completion rates of 60–80% and meaningful reductions in insomnia severity within 6 weeks.

faqs.question: Does alcohol help or hurt sleep?
faqs.answer: Alcohol speeds sleep onset but suppresses REM sleep and causes rebound arousals in the second half of the night, reducing overall sleep quality. Regular use for sleep leads to tolerance requiring higher doses and worsening insomnia on abstinence.

faqs.question: What is paradoxical insomnia?
faqs.answer: Paradoxical insomnia is a mismatch between subjective complaint and objective measurement: patients report sleeping very little while polysomnography shows near-normal sleep duration. It reflects misperception of sleep state and is addressed with CBT-I cognitive components.

metaDescription: Insomnia causes difficulty falling or staying asleep, affecting 10–15% of adults. Learn symptoms, CBT-I therapy, and medication options. Doctor-reviewed.
